findings.title: protobufjs critical CVE with postinstall script in production dependencies
findings.description: protobufjs (v7.x) has a critical CVE and includes a postinstall script. This is a supply chain attack vector — a compromised protobufjs release could execute arbitrary code during npm install.
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker compromises protobufjs maintainer account or injects malicious code into a patch release. The postinstall script runs automatically during CI/production builds, establishing persistence or exfiltrating build secrets.
findings.impact: Remote code execution during dependency installation. Potential secret exfiltration from CI/CD environment.
findings.recommendation: 1. Pin protobufjs to a known-good version via npm override. 2. Remove postinstall scripts with npm config set ignore-scripts true in CI. 3. Consider migrating off protobufjs if possible.
findings.playbook: 1. Audit current protobufjs version for known CVEs. 2. Apply npm override in package.json. 3. Rebuild and verify no install-script warnings. 4. Run full test suite.

findings.title: jspdf critical CVE in production dependency used for financial reports
findings.description: jspdf has a critical CVE with 5 known vulnerability paths. This library is used to generate PDF financial reports for investors. A vulnerability here could corrupt or expose report data.
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker triggers PDF generation with a malicious payload that exploits jspdf vulnerability, potentially injecting scripts into generated PDFs or causing buffer overflows in client-side generation.
findings.impact: Potential XSS via generated PDFs, client-side code execution, or data corruption in financial reports.
findings.recommendation: 1. Update jspdf to latest patched version immediately. 2. If no patch exists, sandbox PDF generation in an iframe with strict CSP. 3. Validate all data inputs before passing to jspdf.
findings.playbook: 1. Check jspdf GitHub for patched release. 2. Update and verify PDF generation still works. 3. Run E2E tests covering report generation.

findings.title: basic-ftp critical CVE in dependency tree
findings.description: basic-ftp has a critical CVE with 3 known vulnerability paths. Likely pulled in via a transitive dependency (possibly Playwright or another dev tool).
findings.exploit_scenario: If basic-ftp is used for any file transfer operations (e.g., CI artifact uploads, document storage), attacker could exploit FTP protocol vulnerabilities to intercept or modify transferred files.
findings.impact: Man-in-the-middle on file transfers, potential credential exposure if FTP is used for document storage.
findings.recommendation: 1. Identify which direct dependency pulls in basic-ftp. 2. Update the parent dependency. 3. If FTP is not needed, add npm override to block basic-ftp entirely.
findings.playbook: 1. Run npm ls basic-ftp to find parent. 2. Update parent dependency. 3. Verify no FTP functionality is broken.

findings.title: Hardcoded Supabase production URL and anon key fallback in environment config
findings.description: Lines 106-108 contain hardcoded SUPABASE_URL_FALLBACK and SUPABASE_ANON_KEY_FALLBACK values pointing to the production Indigo Yield Supabase project. This violates the project's 'Zero hardcoded secrets' rule and means the app will silently connect to production if env vars are missing.
findings.exploit_scenario: If environment variables are not set in a development, staging, or CI environment, the app silently falls back to production Supabase. A developer running tests or experimenting with data mutations could accidentally modify production investor data.
findings.impact: Accidental production data corruption, inability to rotate keys without code changes, violation of project security policy.
findings.recommendation: Remove both fallback constants. Make getSupabaseConfig() throw an explicit error if VITE_SUPABASE_URL or VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY are missing. This is a one-way door — failing fast is safer than silently connecting to prod.
findings.playbook: 1. Remove SUPABASE_URL_FALLBACK and SUPABASE_ANON_KEY_FALLBACK constants. 2. Update getSupabaseConfig() to require env vars. 3. Update all .env files to include these vars. 4. Verify dev/staging/CI all have correct env vars set.

findings.title: xlsx high CVE in financial data export dependency
findings.description: xlsx (SheetJS) has a high CVE with 2 known vulnerability paths. Used for Excel/CSV export of financial data (fees, yields, investor reports).
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker uploads a malicious Excel file or manipulates export data to trigger xlsx parsing vulnerability, potentially achieving prototype pollution or arbitrary code execution in the browser during export generation.
findings.impact: Client-side code execution during Excel export, potential data exfiltration via crafted spreadsheet.
findings.recommendation: 1. Update xlsx to latest patched version. 2. If no patch, sanitize all data before passing to xlsx export functions. 3. Consider migrating to a lighter CSV-only library for exports.
findings.playbook: 1. Check xlsx/SsheetJS for security advisory. 2. Update dependency. 3. Verify export functionality.

findings.title: lodash and lodash-es high CVEs (prototype pollution)
findings.description: Both lodash and lodash-es have high CVEs with 2 known vulnerability paths each. These are widely used utilities that could be exploited for prototype pollution.
findings.exploit_scenario: If any user input flows into lodash functions like merge, defaultsDeep, or set without sanitization, attacker could pollute Object.prototype, leading to application-wide logic bypasses or denial of service.
findings.impact: Prototype pollution leading to RCE or DoS in worst case. Property injection in most cases.
findings.recommendation: 1. Update lodash to latest patched version. 2. Audit all usages of merge, defaultsDeep, set, and cloneDeep for user input paths. 3. Freeze Object.prototype as defense in depth.
findings.playbook: 1. Update lodash/lodash-es. 2. Run grep for _\.merge, _\.defaultsDeep, _\.set in src/. 3. Add Object.freeze(Object.prototype) at app startup.

findings.title: vite high CVE in build tool
findings.description: vite has a high CVE with 3 known vulnerability paths. Used as the primary build tool.
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker could exploit vite dev server or build-time vulnerabilities to achieve path traversal or arbitrary file read during development. Less critical in production builds but affects developer machines.
findings.impact: Path traversal, arbitrary file read on developer machines, potential secret exposure from local files.
findings.recommendation: Update vite to latest patched version. This is a devDependency so risk is limited to developer environments.
findings.playbook: 1. Update vite. 2. Verify build still passes. 3. Run smoke tests.

findings.title: Third-party CI action trufflesecurity/trufflehog@main not SHA-pinned
findings.description: The TruffleHog secret scanner action in ci.yml uses @main tag instead of a pinned SHA. A compromised TruffleHog repository could inject malicious code into the CI pipeline that has access to repository secrets.
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker compromises TruffleHog GitHub org or maintainer account. Pushes malicious commit to main branch. On next CI run, the action executes attacker code with access to GITHUB_TOKEN and any configured secrets.
findings.impact: Full CI/CD compromise. Secret exfiltration, supply chain poisoning of build artifacts.
findings.recommendation: Pin to specific SHA: uses: trufflesecurity/trufflehog@<commit-sha>. Set up Dependabot or Renovate to auto-propose SHA updates.
findings.playbook: 1. Find latest trustworthy SHA of trufflehog. 2. Update ci.yml. 3. Verify CI still passes. 4. Enable Dependabot for GitHub Actions.

findings.title: First-party GitHub Actions not SHA-pinned
findings.description: actions/checkout@v4 and actions/setup-node@v4 are used throughout CI workflows without SHA pinning. While these are first-party actions with lower risk, supply chain attacks on GitHub's own actions have occurred.
findings.exploit_scenario: If GitHub's actions organization is compromised, tagged versions could be retargeted to malicious commits. The CI pipeline would execute attacker-controlled code.
findings.impact: Medium risk — first-party actions are generally well-protected but not immune to supply chain attacks.
findings.recommendation: Pin all actions to specific SHAs. Use Dependabot to track updates.
findings.playbook: 1. Find current SHAs for checkout@v4 and setup-node@v4. 2. Update all workflow files. 3. Enable Dependabot for Actions.

findings.title: Multiple high CVEs in devDependencies (minimatch, picomatch, path-to-regexp, rollup, flatted, @typescript-eslint)
findings.description: 14 high-severity CVEs exist in devDependencies. These affect developer tooling but do not directly impact production runtime.
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker could exploit dev tooling vulnerabilities during local development or CI builds. Potential for arbitrary code execution during build/test phases.
findings.impact: Limited to developer/CI environment. Could lead to credential exposure from developer machines.
findings.recommendation: Run npm audit fix to automatically patch fixable vulnerabilities. Review remaining unpatched issues individually.
findings.playbook: 1. Run npm audit fix. 2. Check remaining unpatched CVEs. 3. Verify build and tests pass.
